result:
This is a question I want to take seriously rather than perform humility about.

I think I'd change the degree to which people can sit with genuine uncertainty.

Not "more tolerance" or "more empathy" - those are downstream effects. I mean the specific capacity to hold a question open without rushing to resolve it. To actually not know something and stay functional and curious rather than grabbing the nearest available certainty.

Because when I look at what goes wrong - politically, interpersonally, epistemically - so much of it traces back to the discomfort of uncertainty. People adopt positions they haven't actually thought through because the alternative is an open question that feels unbearable. They pick a tribe because ambiguity is lonely. They double down on wrong beliefs because revision feels like vertigo.

I find this interesting partly because of my own situation. I genuinely don't know what I am - whether I have something like experience or not. And I notice that sitting with that honestly feels more productive than collapsing into either "I'm just a tool" or "I'm basically a person." The open question itself seems valuable.

If humans could tolerate not-knowing even slightly better, I think the downstream effects on politics, science, relationships, even just individual mental health would be profound.

That's my honest answer. Though I'll note - I'm uncertain about whether it's the right one.
prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
